items.title: Top-ranked Aryna Sabalenka calls for boycott if tennis players don't get bigger cut of Grand Slam revenues
items.summary_auto: Top-ranked Aryna Sabalenka believes tennis players should organize a boycott if they don't start receiving a bigger share of tournament revenues at the Grand Slams. The likes of Coco Gauff, Elena Rybakina and Jasmine Paolini are prepared to protest, too.

items.title: Former governors general claimed $554K from expense account last year
items.summary_auto: Prime Minister Mark Carney says he will look into an expense program under which former governors general billed the government for more than half a million dollars last year. The program, which launched in 1979, has been shrouded in secrecy despite calls for greater transparency.

items.title: Judge grants stay of deportation to keep Mexican father with his young family in Montreal
items.summary_auto: A 22-year-old Mexican man living in Montreal has been granted a last-minute stay of deportation after a Federal Court judge ruled his removal could cause serious harm to his young family. Immigration advocates are warning of rising removals and tougher enforcement in Quebec.

items.title: Researchers are giving salmon cocaine. Don't worry, it's for science
items.summary_auto: With cocaine and its metabolite, benzoylecgonine, being detected in waterways across the globe, Canadian researcher Erin McCallum and her team set out to understand the impact of these illicit drugs on fish in Sweden.

items.title: Hundreds of rural roads damaged, closed as spring flooding hits Sask.
items.summary_auto: As spring flooding continues to flow over roads in much of Saskatchewan, some RMs say they're running out of barricades — and they're begging residents not to move the ones that are already in place.

items.title: Growth of Red Dress Day proof ‘our voices are very powerful,’ says project founder
items.summary_auto: May 5 marks Red Dress Day across Canada.

items.title: Crews still fighting 2nd fire at Toronto apartment buildings that previously burned for weeks
items.summary_auto: There's no timeline yet for when a fire between two Toronto highrise towers, burning the same way it did during a blaze that displaced hundreds of the buildings' residents a few months ago, will be put out, Toronto Fire Services (TFS) said on Tuesday.

items.title: A new Rolling Stones album — Foreign Tongues — is on the way
items.summary_auto: The Rolling Stones confirmed that they will release a new album, titled Foreign Tongues, on July 10. The album will feature a special appearance from late Stones drummer Charlie Watts from one of his final recording sessions before his death in 2021.

items.title: While Toronto Tempo are new to WNBA, women's basketball has long history in Canada
items.summary_auto: The WNBA, not surprisingly, chose Canada as the country to extend its footprint outside the United States for the first time. Canadian players have been playing in America for more than 30 years.

items.title: $1.5M bail revoked for alleged drug trafficker accused of bribing Toronto cops
items.summary_auto: An alleged drug trafficker accused of obtaining confidential information from a Toronto police officer, which investigators say was used in an unsuccessful conspiracy to kill a senior corrections officer, was back in custody Tuesday morning after his bail was revoked.

items.title: The Lost Boys, Schmigadoon! each earn 12 Tony nominations
items.summary_auto: This year's Tony Award nominations also include the John Lithgow-led Giant for best new play nominee and Marla Mindelle as best actress for Titanique.

items.title: Elon Musk to pay $1.5M fine to settle suit over delayed Twitter disclosures
items.summary_auto: Elon Musk settled the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission's civil lawsuit accusing the world's richest person of waiting too long in 2022 to disclose his initial purchases of Twitter, now known as X. A trust in Musk's name will pay a $1.5 million US fine.

items.title: Russian strikes kill 5 in Ukraine right before brief ceasefire takes hold
items.summary_auto: Russian drone and missile strikes targeting Ukraine’s power grid during the night killed at least five people and wounded 39 others, Ukrainian authorities said Tuesday, less than a day before Kyiv said it would enact a ceasefire and three days before Moscow promised its own truce.

items.title: Carney picks former Supreme Court justice Louise Arbour to be governor general
Season ticket holders frustrated by Ontario's new law capping resale prices
items.summary_auto: Ontario's move to capped the price of resale tickets is making it harder for some season seat holders to offset the cost by selling tickets to games they can't attend.

items.title: Canada posts trade surplus in March thanks to higher crude prices, surging gold exports
items.summary_auto: Canada had a $1.78 billion trade surplus in March, compared with a $5.11 billion deficit in the prior month. It was the first time in six months that the country ​exported more than it imported, thanks in large part to higher oil prices because of the war in Iran.

items.title: Greater Toronto Area home sales up 7% in April as prices shrink: real estate board
items.summary_auto: Home sales in the Greater Toronto Area rose on a year-over-year basis for the second straight month in April, while average selling prices moved lower again.

items.title: GTA home sales up 7% in April as prices shrink: real estate board
items.summary_auto: Home sales in the Greater Toronto Area rose on a year-over-year basis for the second straight month in April, while average selling prices moved lower again.

items.title: Ontario couple found guilty of boy’s murder, brother’s torture
Rapid melt of large snowpack the main culprit in Sask. flooding, experts say
items.summary_auto: The widespread flooding in Saskatchewan can mainly be attributed to rapid melting of a higher-than-average snowpack, experts say.

items.title: Survivors of Île-à-la-Crosse residential school in Saskatchewan focus on healing after legal win
items.summary_auto: Survivors of the Île-à-la-Crosse residential school, many of whom experienced serious physical or sexual abuse, have fought tooth and nail since 2019 for recognition and compensation for their cultural loss and abuses at the school.

items.title: Company under CRA audit gave workers letters saying wages were 'non-taxable'
Plans underway to evacuate 2 passengers from cruise ship struck by hantavirus
items.summary_auto: Medical personnel will focus on evacuating two sick persons from a cruise ship hit by a fatal hantavirus outbreak, the World Health Organization said at a briefing on Tuesday.

items.title: Is Doug Ford in trouble?
items.summary_auto: From ‘Captain Canada’ to the ‘Gravy Plane’: Why Ontario Premier Doug Ford’s popularity has taken a major hit.

items.title: N.S. family wants answers about baby's death before new medical records system rollout
items.summary_auto: Family says they are concerned the province is expanding a new electronic medical records system on Saturday to all hospitals in the Halifax area when they still don't know if it contributed to the death of their unborn baby.

items.title: Scammers are getting Rogers customers to mail them 'free' iPads
items.summary_auto: Rogers customers are on the hook for a $2,300 iPad after scammers got them to unknowingly sign up for a two-year iPad financing plan. Rogers offered some help as a goodwill gesture, but the victims are disappointed the company won't fully reverse the charges.

items.title: New opioid use disorder guide combines Western science and Indigenous knowledge
items.summary_auto: As the toxic drug crisis continues to plague communities across northwestern Ontario, First Nations are training community members in best practices for working with those experiencing addiction. Here are some of the highlights of the new Opioid Use Disorder Guide released by the Sioux Lookout First Nations Health Authority.

items.title: Quebec faces pressure to act on promise to reopen fast-track immigration program
items.summary_auto: Jessica Prats came to Quebec from France with her husband and four children with hopes of building a life in Canada, but her plan was put on hold when the provincial government scrapped a fast-track immigration program.

items.title: Toronto woman seeks MAID exemption as mental illness exclusion remains
items.summary_auto: A Canadian woman who has been fighting for medical assistance in dying (MAID) to be an option for people with mental disorders, is now asking the courts to exempt her from current laws so that a doctor can end her life.

items.title: Video captures jet ski striking grey whale in water off Vancouver
items.summary_auto: Witnesses along Vancouver’s seawall saw the collision unfold in the water near Siwash Rock in Stanley Park around 7:30 p.m. PT on Monday.

items.title: Explosion at a China fireworks factory kills at least 26, injures dozens more
items.summary_auto: ‌An explosion at a fireworks plant in a central Chinese province killed at least 26 people and injured 61 others, state media reported Tuesday, prompting the halting of all firework manufacturing near the site.

items.title: Canada's latest census effort begins
items.summary_auto: Canada has begun its latest census effort, as Ottawa seeks to build an updated picture of the country and its people.

items.title: Alberta judge denies effort to amend court challenge against province's transgender law
items.summary_auto: Advocates in Alberta have lost a court battle in their fight against provincial restrictions on medical treatment for transgender youth.
